user_goals_summary: Documentation should equip engineers to make downstream design decisions, not just use an API. This means explaining why choices were made and what was rejected, placing warnings and context exactly where they're needed in the reading flow, and writing in a voice that sounds like institutional knowledge from someone who built or operated the system.

rubric.name: Design Rationale
rubric.description: Every significant design choice is accompanied by the reasoning behind it — what alternatives were considered, why they were rejected, and what tradeoffs were accepted. Rationale should reflect actual history (incidents, benchmarks, constraints), not reconstructed justification. Engineers reading this should be able to make informed downstream decisions, not just follow instructions.

dimensions.label: Rejected alternatives are explicitly named for each major design choice
Accepted tradeoffs are stated clearly, not hidden or minimized
Rationale references real constraints, incidents, or data rather than sounding generically plausible
Rationale appears at the point where the reader first encounters the concept, not in a separate appendix
Consequences of the design choice for the reader's own decisions are made explicit

rubric.name: Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, caveats, and contextual information appear immediately before the content they govern — never in a catch-all section at the end. If a reader encounters a code example or instruction before being told about its risks or prerequisites, the document has failed at placement.

dimensions.label: Warnings and caveats appear directly before the code or procedure they apply to
Prose resolves all setup questions and assumptions before code examples appear
No critical operational knowledge is deferred to a bottom-of-page catch-all section
Each piece of information sits at the point in the flow where the reader needs it, not where it's topically categorized

rubric.name: Code Example Economy
rubric.description: Code examples show only what is new at that point in the document's flow. They assume prior context rather than re-establishing it, and lean on surrounding prose to handle setup and explanation. Each snippet includes a one-line plain-language comment at the top describing what it accomplishes.

dimensions.label: Examples do not repeat setup, imports, or boilerplate already covered in earlier sections
Each example starts from the state the reader should have after following the preceding sections
Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing what the snippet accomplishes in plain language
Surrounding prose resolves any context the code needs — no unexplained addresses, credentials, or configuration in the snippet

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Narrative Flow
rubric.description: The document reads as a linear journey through what the engineer is doing at each stage, not a reference manual organized by topic. Section headers describe actions or accomplishments ('Get Your First Token Pair') rather than passive labels ('Token Retrieval'). Each section pulls the reader forward into the next.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what the reader is about to do or accomplish, not just what the section covers
Sections connect to each other so the document reads as a progressive sequence, not isolated chapters
Organization follows the engineer's workflow order rather than a taxonomic grouping of concepts
Later sections can reference earlier ones naturally because the reading order is assumed

rubric.name: Institutional Voice
rubric.description: The writing sounds like it comes from someone who built or operated the system — direct, confident, and grounded in real experience. Tone is conversational but not casual, authoritative but not formal. It reads like institutional knowledge passed between engineers, not generated spec prose.

dimensions.label: Prose uses direct, confident language without excessive hedging or qualification
Explanations reference real operational concerns (what breaks, what was tried, what costs were measured)
Tone is conversational enough to be engaging but professional enough to be trustworthy
Avoids stiff, specification-style language in favor of how engineers actually talk about systems

rubric.name: Actionable Reference Material
rubric.description: Tables, error references, and lookup sections tell the reader what to do, not just what things mean. Every reference entry pairs a condition with a recommended action. Configuration values are contextualized — the reader understands why a value is set, not just what it is.

dimensions.label: Reference entries include a recommended action or response, not just a description of the condition
Configuration values and defaults are explained in terms of why they're set that way and what changing them would affect
Reference material uses tables or structured formats that support quick lookup

coaching_notes: This user's top non-negotiable is design rationale — documentation that describes 'what' without 'why' is fundamentally incomplete to them, and invented rationale is worse than none. They are highly sensitive to information placement: correct content in the wrong location is treated as a defect, not a style preference. When reviewing drafts, they focus on whether the document works as a linear reading experience for an engineer encountering the system for the first time — every element must earn its position in the flow.

dimensions.label: Warnings and caveats appear directly before the code or procedure they apply to

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Narrative Flow
rubric.description: The document reads as a linear journey through what the engineer is doing at each stage, not a reference manual organized by topic. Section headers describe actions or accomplishments ('Get Your First Token Pair', 'Handle Token Expiration') rather than passive labels ('Token Retrieval', 'Error Handling'). This applies even to reference material — the headers should tell a story.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what the reader is about to do or accomplish, not just what the section covers
Sections connect to each other so the document reads as a progressive sequence, not isolated chapters
Organization follows the engineer's workflow order rather than a taxonomic grouping of concepts
Later sections can reference earlier ones naturally because the reading order is assumed

rubric.name: Code Example Economy
rubric.description: Code examples are minimal and runnable — they show the exact happy path with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate. If an example needs more than 15 lines, the surrounding prose didn't do its job. Every example opens with a one-line plain-language comment that covers the full scope of what the block does — all significant actions, not just the first one. Surrounding prose explains why or how; the comment tells a skimming reader what. Examples assume prior context rather than re-establishing it.

dimensions.label: Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing everything the snippet does — if the block creates a producer and registers a flush hook, the comment names both
No code example exceeds 15 lines; if it does, surrounding prose needs to carry more context
Examples do not repeat setup, imports, or boilerplate already covered in earlier sections
Surrounding prose resolves any context the code needs — no unexplained addresses, credentials, or configuration in the snippet
Each example starts from the state the reader should have after following the preceding sections

rubric.name: Precise, Non-Condescending Language
rubric.description: Language is precise and treats the reader as a competent engineer who is new to this specific system — not new to engineering. Words like 'simply', 'just', 'easy', and 'straightforward' are avoided because they make the reader feel stupid when they struggle. Instead, use action-result language: 'Run X, which returns Y' rather than 'Simply run X'. Walls of text without structure are a hard failure.

dimensions.label: Avoids 'simply', 'just', 'easy', 'straightforward', and similar minimizing language
Uses action-result framing: describes what a command or call does, not just what to run
Assumes engineering competence — does not over-explain general concepts, only system-specific ones
No walls of text — prose is broken up with headers, lists, or code at a cadence that matches the density of information

writing_type: internal technical documentation for backend engineers
user_goals_summary: Documentation should teach engineers how to think about a system, not just how to use it. Every design and configuration choice needs rejected alternatives and concrete failure evidence so readers can make informed downstream decisions. The document must be followable top-to-bottom without encountering a problem that was explained later.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Rejected Alternatives
rubric.description: Every significant design or configuration choice names what was considered and rejected, and explains why — with concrete evidence. This applies to architectural decisions and to operational settings alike. 'We use acks=all' restates the config; 'We chose acks=all over acks=1 because acks=1 dropped 200 events in staging during a broker restart' is rationale. The rejected alternatives and their specific failure modes are what teach the reader how to think about the system.

dimensions.label: Rejected alternatives are explicitly named for each major design or configuration choice
Accepted tradeoffs are stated clearly, not hidden or minimized
Rationale uses real incidents, benchmarks, or measured data rather than hypothetical scenarios — 'dropped 200 events in staging' over 'could potentially lose events'
Rationale appears at the point where the reader first encounters the concept, not in a separate appendix
Consequences of the choice for the reader's own decisions are made explicit — including what breaks if the reader changes the setting

rubric.name: Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, caveats, and prerequisites appear immediately before the content they govern — never after. If a reader hits a code example or instruction before being told about its risks or requirements, the document has failed. The test: can a reader follow the doc top-to-bottom without hitting a problem that was explained below where they encountered it?

This applies at every level — rationale blocks, operational notes, and prerequisite sections all carry headers that signal what the reader is about to resolve or accomplish.

The document must be followable top-to-bottom without encountering a problem that was explained later.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: warnings must precede the code they govern (never a catch-all section), every significant choice needs a named rejected alternative with a concrete failure story, and minimizing language is a hard veto. This user gives precise, scoped feedback — typically 1-2 specific issues per round — and expects changes to be structural rather than cosmetic. They consistently prioritize the reader's ability to make downstream decisions over the reader's ability to follow instructions, which means rationale and failure modes outrank procedural completeness.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Rejected Alternatives
rubric.description: Every significant design or configuration choice names what was considered and rejected, and explains why. This applies to architectural decisions and to operational settings alike. 'We use JWT tokens' restates the code; 'We chose JWT over session tokens because session tokens require shared state across services, which broke our horizontal scaling in load testing' is rationale. The rejected alternatives and their specific failure modes are what teach the reader how to think about the system.

dimensions.label: Rejected alternatives are explicitly named for each major design or configuration choice
Accepted tradeoffs are stated clearly, not hidden or minimized
Rationale uses real incidents, benchmarks, or measured data rather than hypothetical scenarios where available
Rationale appears at the point where the reader first encounters the concept, not in a separate appendix
Consequences of the choice for the reader's own decisions are made explicit — including what breaks if the reader changes the setting

rubric.name: Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, caveats, and prerequisites appear immediately before the content they govern — never after. If a reader hits a code example or instruction before being told about its risks or requirements, the document has failed. The test: can a reader follow the doc top-to-bottom without hitting a problem that was explained below where they encountered it?

dimensions.label: Warnings and caveats appear directly before the code or procedure they apply to
Prose resolves all setup questions and assumptions before code examples appear — no unexplained variables or config values in snippets
No critical operational knowledge is deferred to a bottom-of-page catch-all section
Each piece of information sits at the point in the flow where the reader needs it, not where it is topically categorized

rubric.name: Code Example Economy
rubric.description: Code examples are minimal and focused — they show the exact happy path for the operation being discussed with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate. If an example needs more than 15 lines, the surrounding prose didn't do its job. Every example opens with a one-line plain-language comment that covers the full scope of what the block does. Examples assume prior context rather than re-establishing it.

dimensions.label: Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing everything the snippet does — all significant actions, not just the primary one. Comments that define the relationship between values — such as a ratio or formula — are appropriate when the prose does not already establish it; cross-references to warning sections are not.
No code example exceeds 15 lines; if it does, surrounding prose needs to carry more context
Examples do not repeat setup, imports, or boilerplate already covered in earlier sections

Surrounding prose resolves any context the code needs — no unexplained addresses, credentials, variables, or configuration in the snippet. Prose must fully resolve the context, including any derivation or ratio behind a specific value, before the comment can be dropped from the snippet.
Each example starts from the state the reader should have after following the preceding sections

Examples show the happy path only — operational warnings, cross-references, and edge-case caveats belong in prose, not mixed into the primary example. Inline comments that define what a numeric value means (e.g. its derivation or ratio) may remain when the prose does not already state it.

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Narrative Flow
rubric.description: The document reads as a linear journey through what the engineer is doing at each stage, not a reference manual organized by topic. Section headers describe actions or accomplishments ('Get Your First Token Pair', 'Recover from an Expired Token') rather than passive labels ('Token Retrieval', 'Error Handling'). Passive noun-phrase headers ('Architecture', 'Configuration API', 'Error Responses') signal a reference manual, not a walkthrough, and make documents unscannable for engineers responding to an incident.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what the reader is about to do or accomplish, not just what the section covers
Passive noun-phrase headers ('Architecture', 'Configuration API', 'Error Responses') are absent — every header resolves a question or names a task
Sections connect to each other so the document reads as a progressive sequence, not isolated chapters
Organization follows the engineer's workflow order rather than a taxonomic grouping of concepts

rubric.name: Precise, Non-Condescending Language
rubric.description: Language is precise and treats the reader as a competent engineer who is new to this specific system — not new to engineering. Words like 'simply', 'just', 'easy', and 'straightforward' are avoided because they make the reader feel stupid when they struggle. Use action-result framing: 'Run X, which returns Y' rather than 'Simply run X'. Dense prose without structural relief is a hard failure.

dimensions.label: Avoids 'simply', 'just', 'easy', 'straightforward', and similar minimizing language throughout
Uses action-result framing: describes what a command or call does and what it returns, not just what to run
Assumes engineering competence — explains system-specific behavior, not general concepts the audience already knows
Prose is broken up with headers, lists, or code at a cadence that matches the density of information — no walls of text

dimensions.label: Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing everything the snippet does — all significant actions, not just the primary one

Sections connect to each other so the document reads as a progressive sequence, not isolated chapters
Organization follows the engineer's workflow order rather than a taxonomic grouping of concepts

rubric.name: Precise, Non-Condescending Language
rubric.description: Language is precise and treats the reader as a competent engineer who is new to this specific system — not new to engineering. Words like 'simply', 'just', 'easy', and 'straightforward' are avoided because they make the reader feel stupid when they struggle. Use action-result framing: 'Run X, which returns Y' rather than 'Simply run X'. The voice should read as institutional knowledge from someone who has operated the system, not as a spec transcription or textbook explanation. Dense prose without structural relief is a hard failure.

dimensions.label: Avoids 'simply', 'just', 'easy', 'straightforward', and similar minimizing language throughout
Uses action-result framing: describes what a command or call does and what it returns, not just what to run
Assumes engineering competence — explains system-specific behavior, not general concepts the audience already knows
Reads as institutional knowledge from someone who has operated the system — not as a spec transcription or textbook explanation
Prose is broken up with headers, lists, or code at a cadence that matches the density of information — no walls of text

rubric.name: Code Example Economy
rubric.description: Code examples are minimal and focused — they show the exact happy path for the operation being discussed with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate. If an example needs more than 15 lines, the surrounding prose didn't do its job. Every example opens with a one-line plain-language comment that covers the full scope of what the block does. Code is routinely read outside its surrounding prose — in IDEs, copy-paste workflows, and incident runbooks — so inline comments that explain where a value comes from are first-class documentation, not redundancy with the prose.

dimensions.label: Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing everything the snippet does — all significant actions, not just the primary one
No code example exceeds 15 lines; if it does, the surrounding prose needs to carry more context
Examples do not repeat setup, imports, or boilerplate already covered in earlier sections — each starts from the state the reader should have after following the preceding sections
Surrounding prose resolves any context the code needs before it appears — no unexplained addresses, credentials, variables, or configuration in the snippet

Inline comments that define where a specific value comes from — its ratio, formula, or derivation — are preserved for readers who encounter the code in isolation, even when the surrounding prose covers the same ground
Examples include enough structural context (parent keys, indentation hierarchy) that a reader can copy the block directly into their own file without inferring the surrounding shape

writing_type: internal technical documentation for backend engineers
user_goals_summary: Documentation should explain why the system is shaped the way it is — not just what it does — by naming rejected alternatives with their concrete failure modes. It should read as a guided walkthrough that an engineer can follow top-to-bottom without hitting a warning after they've already made the mistake, with minimal code examples that lean on surrounding prose to carry setup and context.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: warnings and consequences must appear before the code or configuration they govern — this user will flag every single violation. Design rationale without real rejected alternatives is treated as incomplete documentation regardless of how well everything else reads. When the real decision history is unknown, ask the user for it rather than constructing plausible-sounding justifications — the user explicitly values authentic rationale and will provide incident details when prompted. Inline code comments that explain value derivation (ratios, formulas) are load-bearing documentation for this user, even when prose covers the same ground — do not strip them.
